text: This is a 54 year old woman with a squamous cell carcinoma at the distal portion of the main left bronchus.
We chose a single port approach for a left pneumonectomy.
As expected, the lung has complete atelectasis.
There is a lot of edema around the pulmonary hilum and there is no obvious plane of dissection.
The tissues are thicker, so it is technically more challenge to be able to dissect and identify the vascular structures of the hilum.
With thoracoscopic instruments and cautery, we start by dissecting the uppermost part of the hilum to expose the pulmonary artery and the AP window.
Through single pore approach, this procedure can be safely performed because of the straight vision that we have to the hilum.
Keeping always certain traction over the lung, we have better exposure, allowing for a safe dissection.
Progressively, we identify all the vascular structures of the hilum.
This big lymphadenopathy is intimately attached to the superior pulmonary vein and laterally to the pulmonary artery.
With the help of the cautery and with some traction, we're able to find the appropriate plane for dissection.
This is a station 10 and station 5 lymphadenopathy.
Once freed from the vascular structures, care is taken not to harm the laryngeal recurrent nerve.
We put some traction away from the pulmonary window to have a better exposure.
We continue to dissect our way towards the posterior portion of the main pulmonary artery, trying to find a good plane of the dissection on the back wall between the pulmonary artery and the aorta.
Next, we move back to the anterior portion.
Now we are dissecting between the pulmonary artery and the superior pulmonary vein, trying to find a good plane of dissection.
Again, a lot of inflamed tissues surrounding the vascular structures.
With the help of a thoracoscopic dissector, we try to find the plane between the main pulmonary artery and the vein.

the main pulmonary artery.
This procedure will certainly decrease the risk of any vascular accident during this dissection.
Once the pulmonary artery is maximally freed, we again use thoracoscopic instruments to dissect behind of the back wall of the main pulmonary artery towards the aorta with a right angle
we find our plane of dissection and we are able to fully and completely surround the main pulmonary artery, the main left pulmonary artery.
This is encircled with a vascular loop.
We continue the dissection towards the pulmonary vein with the help of some traction over the pericardium and over the lung.
This way we can better expose the vascular structures.
Again, with the cautery, we dissect the very thick tissue that is covering the superior vein.
And finally, we're able to have a good exposure
of the superior pulmonary vein.
Absence of lymphatic metastasis is confirmed.
We proceed to the ligation and transection of the main pulmonary artery with the help of a stapler with a vascular load.
we try to transect the pulmonary artery at the base so we can have a larger oncologic margin.
Subsequently, we continue our dissection of the pulmonary vein
We try to dissect with the help of a thoracoscopic dissector the vein from the bronchus.
This is a very safe plane to dissect.
It is again very important to free as much as possible of the superior pulmonary vein.

Once the superior pulmonary vein is transected, we continue our dissection towards the main left bronchus.

There is no sign of neoplastic transgression.
We now advance to the inferior portion at the level of the pulmonary ligament.

so we can safely exposure the vessel.

This part of the operation was a little bit tedious because of all this very thick tissue, but safely and progressively, we were able to dissect without any bleeding or any other complication.
Once the plane between the left main bronchus and the inferior pulmonary vein is developed, we can easily find our plane of dissection
and fully encircle the inferior pulmonary vein.
We can now have a clear view of the inferior pulmonary vein, still some thick tissue surrounding.
We're able to fully dissect the vessel with a good exposure.
And with the stapler and a vascular load, we transect the inferior pulmonary vein.
We now proceed to the final stage of this operation.
We move towards the main left bronchus.
We are dissecting towards the subcarinal region where we can clearly see, once again, important adenopathy.
These lymph nodes seem to be inflammatory lymph nodes.

Care is taken not to burn the airway during this dissection, so we try to keep it as close as possible to the lymph nodes instead of the airway.
So there's a big station 7 lymphadenopathy, which is completely dissected and sent for frozen analysis.
Once again, we confirm the absence of metastatic disease.
Once we complete the subcarinal dissection, we have now completely exposed the main left bronchus medially and posteriorly.

With the help of an umbilical cord, we surround the left main bronchus and we put some traction over it to facilitate the complete dissection on the anterior and lateral wall of the main left bronchus.
It is very important to fully liberate this bronchus from all its attachments.
Once completely free, we put some traction so we can access its most proximal portion.
This is a very important maneuver to have a minimal stump.
The stapler is passed towards the aorta.
It is important to stay above the aorta and put some pressure downward at the same time the bronchus is being pulled upward.
We perform this at least two to three times to be as far down as possible from the endobronchial tumor.
To complete the procedure, we perform the intercostal nerve block with Marca-N.

through the same thoracoscopic incision.
